title: The Dealership
weakest_section: 16:15-17:00 with old man confusion and setup moments

jokes.text: When are they gonna have the flying cars? Yeah, they have been promising that. Years. When we were kids it seemed like it was around the corner.
jokes.setup: The eternal promise of futuristic flying cars from childhood
jokes.punchline: The disappointment that they're still not here after decades
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor about shared cultural disappointments. Well-crafted setup about childhood expectations vs. reality.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I realized? We were promised flying cars. Remember? When we were kids, flying cars were supposed to be here by now.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know! I've been waiting my whole life for a flying car.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And nothing! We got the internet, we got cell phones, we got... we got nothing that matters. No flying cars.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I would've settled for just one flying car. Just to see it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We're never getting them, George. It's not happening. We're stuck on the ground like animals.

jokes.text: I think Ed Begley Jr. has one. No, that's just electric.
jokes.setup: Suggestion that Ed Begley Jr. has a flying car
jokes.punchline: Deflation that it's just electric (referencing his environmental activism)
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection playing on Ed Begley Jr's environmental reputation. Economical and well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, Ed Begley Jr. has a flying car?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's what I heard.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A flying car? Ed Begley Jr.?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, why?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, it's electric.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? It doesn't fly. It just... hovers three inches off the ground at two miles per hour.

jokes.text: The government is very touchy about us being in the air. Let us run on the ground much as we want. Anything in the air is a big production.
jokes.setup: Government restrictions on flying
jokes.punchline: The contrast between freedom on ground vs. strict air control
jokes.explanation: Good Jerry-style observational humor with absurdist logic. The 'big production' phrasing is quintessentially Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's interesting how we talk about freedom in this country. On the ground, you can do whatever you want. You can walk around, you can run, you can skip, you can do cartwheels down the street. Nobody cares. But the moment you get in an airplane, suddenly the government has all these rules. 'You have to sit down. You have to keep your seatbelt fastened. You can't move around.' It's like, 'Hey, I thought this was America! I'm 30,000 feet in the air and I have less freedom than I do on the ground!'

jokes.text: Would you stop it? You'll have plenty of time to destroy it after I get it.
jokes.setup: Kramer presumably examining/touching Jerry's potential new car
jokes.punchline: Jerry's resigned acceptance that Kramer will inevitably destroy it
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - Jerry knows Kramer well enough to predict future destruction. The resigned tone makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Nice car, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, I could really use a car like this.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you can't have it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. Just let me take it for a spin.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No. Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because the last time I let you borrow something, you returned it in pieces.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That was one time!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was my good watch.

jokes.text: Hey, George, I'm buying this car. What is wrong with you? You never tell them you like the car.
jokes.setup: Jerry announces he's buying the car
jokes.punchline: George's horror at Jerry's negotiating naivety
jokes.explanation: Classic George paranoia about getting screwed over. Perfect character integration showing George's neurotic expertise.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm buying the car.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're buying the car? Just like that? You're not gonna negotiate?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What's to negotiate? He's got a price, I pay it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You don't negotiate with a car dealer! That's like going to a hooker and paying the asking price!

jokes.text: That vein again.
jokes.setup: George getting worked up about car buying
jokes.punchline: Jerry's casual observation of George's stress vein appearing
jokes.explanation: Economical callback to George's stress vein. Sets up recurring gag for the episode.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't believe they want that much for this car! It's insane! Do you know what I could do with that money?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, and look — there's your stress vein.

jokes.text: Easy move. Go from screwing you behind your back... to screwing you right to your face.
jokes.setup: Puddy's career change from mechanic to salesman
jokes.punchline: The progression from hidden to open customer exploitation
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay on 'screwing' with perfect parallel structure. Very quotable and cynically brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So Puddy's a car salesman now? That's perfect for him.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why is that perfect?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because as a mechanic, he was ripping people off secretly. Now he can do it right out in the open.

jokes.text: They stick you with the undercoating, rustproofing, dealer prep. Suddenly you're on your back like a turtle.
jokes.setup: List of car dealer scams
jokes.punchline: The turtle metaphor for helplessness
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with vivid turtle metaphor. The image of helpless flailing is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Next thing I know, I'm getting dropped off in a Le Car with a fabric sunroof. All the kids are shouting at me, 'Hey, Le George. Bonjour, Le George. Let's stuff Le George in Le Locker.'
jokes.setup: George's father's car salesman buddy was supposed to help
jokes.punchline: The humiliating French car and resulting childhood trauma
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from car deal to childhood trauma. 'Le George' is perfect comedic naming and the whole sequence is highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My father's friend was supposed to help me get a car. Instead, he sold my father this ridiculous French sedan.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Do you know what it's like to be a kid and have your father pull up in a French car? The other kids laughed at me for years.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm still in therapy because of that car.

jokes.text: Maybe I should talk to him. Oh, I don't think so. No, he's an entertainer. You know, all over the place. That's where I come in. I see. So you're his manag...? Neighbour. That's right.
jokes.setup: Rick assumes Kramer is Jerry's manager
jokes.punchline: The deflation to 'neighbour'
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Kramer building up importance then deflating. Classic Kramer logic.

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: So you're Jerry's manager?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, I'm just a neighbor.

jokes.text: Look at these salesmen. The only thing these guys fear is the walkout. No matter what they say, you say: 'I'll walk out of here right now.' Can I help you with something? Hold it. One more step and we're walking!
jokes.setup: George explaining walkout strategy
jokes.punchline: Immediately overreacting to innocent salesman approach
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff with George immediately applying his paranoid strategy inappropriately. Great escalation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You see, the key is the clammy hands. It's a good non-verbal way of saying, 'I'm anxious, but I'm willing to do this.'

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Can I help you?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes! Yes, you can help me! Why are you touching me?! Get your hands off me! I know what you're doing!

jokes.text: My new salesman boyfriend took me out to celebrate his promotion. Oh, where'd you go? To a restaurant. Arby's.
jokes.setup: Romantic celebration dinner expectation
jokes.punchline: The deflating reality of Arby's
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection from 'restaurant' to 'Arby's.' The pause before 'Arby's' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, this is romantic! Candlelight, soft music...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, don't get too excited. I made reservations at Arby's.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Arby's? That's not romantic!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? They have the roast beef sandwiches. It's like a five-star meal if you close your eyes and think really hard.

jokes.text: I had the roast beef.
jokes.setup: Discussion of romantic celebration dinner
jokes.punchline: Puddy's matter-of-fact menu choice
jokes.explanation: Puddy's deadpan delivery makes this mundane detail funny. Shows his character's simplicity.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: I'm getting the salmon.

jokes.text: So I decided I'm gonna go with a 900 convertible. All right, classic. High-five.
jokes.setup: Jerry's car choice
jokes.punchline: Puddy's inappropriate high-five request
jokes.explanation: Sets up Puddy's annoying high-five habit. Character-specific behavior that will escalate.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm thinking about getting a new car.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Yeah? What kind?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, maybe a Saab.

dialogue.speaker: Pudty
dialogue.line: A Saab? High five!

jokes.text: Well, it's a test drive, right? I never drive around here. If I'm gonna recommend this car... I need to see that it'll handle my daily routine. Well, where're we going? Just a little place I like to call 'You'll See.'
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining need to test car on his route
jokes.punchline: The mysterious destination 'You'll See'
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer mysterious behavior. 'You'll See' as a destination name is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I gotta test drive this car on my route.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You'll see.

jokes.text: If you have to speak, mumble. Au revoir, Le George. Don't think it can't happen.
jokes.setup: George's negotiating advice
jokes.punchline: Jerry mockingly using the Le George reference, George's paranoid response
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to earlier Le George story. George's paranoid 'don't think it can't happen' is perfectly neurotic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, I'm supposed to go in there and say 'Le George'?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Not 'Le George,' just George. But the way you say it — it's like you're doing me a favor.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, I see. So it's not about the name, it's about the attitude.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! And don't smile like that — they can see you through the glass!

jokes.text: Puddy, this is a pretty good move for you. No more grease monkey. I don't care for that term. Oh, sorry, I didn't know. I don't know too many monkeys who could take apart a fuel injector.
jokes.setup: Jerry apologizing for 'grease monkey' term
jokes.punchline: Puddy's literal defense of monkey intelligence
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - expected PC sensitivity but got literal monkey defense. Very Puddy-like literal thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I didn't mean anything by 'grease monkey.' It's just a figure of speech.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Well, you know, monkeys are actually very intelligent animals. They can solve problems, use tools...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know, but—

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: So if a monkey can get greasy working on cars, that's actually pretty impressive.

jokes.text: I saw one that could do sign language. Yeah, I saw that one. Koko. Yeah, Koko. Right. Koko. That chimp's all right. High-five.
jokes.setup: Discussion of intelligent monkeys
jokes.punchline: Bonding over Koko leading to another high-five request
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation of monkey discussion leading to inevitable high-five. Builds the running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, you know about Koko the gorilla?

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Yeah, she's great. She understands sign language, she's got a kitten she takes care of...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, she's incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: We should high-five on that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no more high-fives.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, hey. What's going on here? You didn't agree to anything. No, we both just saw the same monkey.
jokes.setup: George seeing Jerry and Puddy bonding
jokes.punchline: Jerry's innocent explanation about the monkey
jokes.explanation: George's paranoia about Jerry getting too friendly with the salesman, deflated by innocent monkey discussion.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What's going on here? You two are like best friends now.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We were just talking about the monkey at the zoo.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The monkey?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, Puddy was saying how the monkey kept staring at him, and I said maybe the monkey thought he was a monkey too.

jokes.text: Hey, where's Puddy? The copy machine is broken. That's what they want you to think.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking about broken copy machine
jokes.punchline: Jerry's conspiracy theory about deliberate sabotage
jokes.explanation: Classic conspiracy theory humor. Jerry's paranoia about dealership tactics is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: The copy machine is broken again.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Broken? It's not broken, it's being sabotaged. Think about it — every time you need to make a copy, it jams. Every single time. That's not a malfunction, that's a conspiracy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, it's just a machine.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's what they want you to think. Who benefits from a broken copy machine? The copy machine company — they sell more machines. It's all connected.

jokes.text: Slapping hands is the lowest form of male primate ritual. In fact, some of them have moved on. They're doing sign language now. It's that bad? What do you think the Nazis were doing? That was the heil-five.
jokes.setup: Jerry's disdain for high-fives as primitive behavior
jokes.punchline: Connecting Nazi salute to high-five as 'heil-five'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy escalation from harmless high-five criticism to Nazi comparison. 'Heil-five' is inspired wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The high-five is just a primitive gesture. It's like a Nazi salute, except instead of 'Heil Hitler,' it's 'Heil-five!'

jokes.text: Isn't that from your act, like, 10 years ago? It was a good bit in the '80s, and it's still relatable today.
jokes.setup: Elaine calling out Jerry's recycled material
jokes.punchline: Jerry's defensive justification of his old material
jokes.explanation: Great meta-humor about comedians recycling material. Very self-aware for the show.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You told that joke about the airplane peanuts five years ago!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? It's still funny. Besides, comedy is timeless. A good joke never gets old.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, you can't just recycle material. You have to come up with new stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? People forget. And even if they remember, they want to hear it again. It's like a song you love—you don't stop listening to it just because you've heard it before.

jokes.text: Good news, we got a 900 in black. That's the hot colour. High-five.
jokes.setup: Puddy delivering good news
jokes.punchline: Another inappropriate high-five request
jokes.explanation: Continues the high-five running gag but doesn't add much new. Functional but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Your girlfriend's been cleared by her doctor. You can have relations again.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great news!

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: High five!

jokes.text: When your wallet was open, I glanced inside... and I couldn't help but notice that you have several crisp dollar bills. You're incorrect.
jokes.setup: George desperately needing change for vending machine
jokes.punchline: Mechanic's flat denial despite obvious evidence
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of George's desperation meeting stubborn refusal. 'You're incorrect' is perfectly dismissive.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You got change for a dollar?

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You don't have change for a dollar?

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: No, I don't.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How can you not have change for a dollar? You're a mechanic. You work on cars. You must make hundreds of dollars a day.

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: I don't have change.

jokes.text: I guess everyone here enjoys giving the old scroogie, huh? You're all doing a hell of a job.
jokes.setup: George's mounting frustration with dealership staff
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic 'scroogie' comment and fake praise
jokes.explanation: Perfect George escalation with his invented term 'scroogie' and bitter sarcasm. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, that's beautiful. You know what? You're a very nice man. You know that? You're really nice. I mean, I don't know what I was thinking before.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're scroogie? What kind of a name is Scroogie? That's a great name though, really. Scroogie!

jokes.text: Hey, ladies. It's a Saab 900. What do you think? Can I interest you in a little supplemental restraint?
jokes.setup: Kramer trying to impress women with the car
jokes.punchline: The suggestive 'supplemental restraint' line
jokes.explanation: Kramer turning car safety features into pickup lines. Characteristic boundary-crossing behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You see, this car has a supplemental restraint system.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, if we're driving and I have to stop suddenly, these straps will hold you in place so you don't go flying.

jokes.text: People drop change down here, Jerry. And they're too lazy to pick it up. Either that or they've got a weird little hang-up about lying face down in filth.
jokes.setup: George explaining his change-hunting under vending machines
jokes.punchline: The 'weird hang-up about lying face down in filth'
jokes.explanation: Great George logic - normalizing his desperate behavior by criticizing others' hygiene standards. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I got over it. I was lying face down in the dirt outside a vending machine, and I realized I had hit rock bottom. And I thought, 'You know, this is it. This is the lowest point of my life.' And I thought, 'It can't get any worse.' So, it didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're okay with it now?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I still have a weird hang-up about lying face down in filth.

jokes.text: He's got a live one. He's just reeling this big fish in.
jokes.setup: Observing Puddy selling to Jerry
jokes.punchline: The fishing metaphor for sales manipulation
jokes.explanation: Standard fishing metaphor for sales, but George's paranoid perspective makes it work. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like fishing. You cast your line out there, wait for a bite. The fish doesn't know he's being caught.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So the customer is the fish?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Of course! And Puddy, he's reeling you in.

jokes.text: Hey, could I have my dollar back? It's wrinkled. It's worthless.
jokes.setup: George asking for his dollar back
jokes.punchline: Jerry's ridiculous excuse about wrinkled money
jokes.explanation: Absurd excuse for not returning money. Shows how the dealership environment corrupts everyone.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I want my dollar back.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can't give it back to you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because I already spent it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You spent my dollar?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, it got all wrinkled up. I didn't think you'd want it back looking like that.

jokes.text: Well, just one more errand and we can head back. Actually, it looks like we're gonna need some gas. Well, how much gas you think is in there right now? Well, it's on E.
jokes.setup: Kramer's extended test drive reaching gas crisis
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that they're on empty
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Kramer's test drive abuse. Sets up the gas gauge subplot nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So I'm on this test drive, right? I'm cruising around, feeling good...

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: How long have you been out there?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I dunno, few hours. But here's the thing - I haven't looked at the gas gauge.

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: You haven't looked at the gas gauge?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Not once. I've just been enjoying the ride.

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: Kramer, you should probably check that.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah... Oh. We're on empty.

jokes.text: Hey, it's gone. Where is my Twix? That guy's eating it. How do you know that one's yours? It was dangling. There were two left in the machine. He must have bought one and gotten both.
jokes.setup: George's Twix bar disappeared from vending machine
jokes.punchline: His detailed investigation and accusation
jokes.explanation: Perfect George obsession escalation. The detailed logic about dangling candy shows his neurotic attention to perceived injustice.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Where's my Twix? I put my money in, I pressed the button, nothing came out. So I pressed it again.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A Twix falls out. I pick it up, I'm satisfied. I go back an hour later, the Twix is gone!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Someone took my Twix out of that machine. And I know who it was.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was Kramer. He's the only one who knew about it. He probably came down here, saw my Twix, and took it.

jokes.text: High-five. On the flip side. David, l... Don't leave me hanging.
jokes.setup: Another high-five request
jokes.punchline: Puddy's 'on the flip side' and 'don't leave me hanging' variations
jokes.explanation: Escalates the high-five gag with new variations. Shows Puddy's complete commitment to this annoying habit.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: High five!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Come on, on the flip side.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Pudty
dialogue.line: Don't leave me hanging.

jokes.text: You're a salesman now. The high-five is... It's very grease monkey. What did I tell you about that? I'm sorry. But the high-five is just so stupid. Oh, yeah? I'll tell you what's stupid. You, stupid.
jokes.setup: Elaine criticizing Puddy's high-five habit
jokes.punchline: Puddy's incredibly childish 'You, stupid' response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Puddy's regression to playground insults when challenged. 'You, stupid' is hilariously immature.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You can't just high-five everybody. You're not a golden retriever.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: You, stupid.

jokes.text: High-five, on the flip side.
jokes.setup: Puddy breaking up with Elaine
jokes.punchline: Ending the relationship with a high-five reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that turns the running gag into relationship ammunition. Very petty and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I think we should see other people.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: I don't know, I just think it's time.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But... we're good together.

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Yeah, but... high five!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: Keys? How are you gonna start it?
jokes.setup: Puddy listing ridiculous car charges including keys
jokes.punchline: Jerry's logical question about starting the car without keys
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - charging separately for keys reveals the insanity of dealer add-ons. Perfectly economical.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, how do you start the car without keys?

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: You push the button.

jokes.text: First of all, it wasn't a Twix. It was a 5th Avenue bar. You must think I'm pretty stupid. That's no 5th Avenue bar. I can see the crumb in the corner of your lip. Now, that is a cookie and we all know that Twix is the only candy bar with the cookie crunch.
jokes.setup: Mechanic denying he ate George's Twix
jokes.punchline: George's forensic candy analysis
jokes.explanation: Exceptional escalation with George becoming a candy forensics expert. The cookie crunch detail is perfect product knowledge turned obsessive.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You ate my Twix!

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: I did not eat your Twix.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: There's chocolate on your shirt!

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: I like chocolate, I eat chocolate all the time.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Not Twix chocolate. This is a very specific chocolate. I can tell by the nougat density.

jokes.text: Nougat? Please. I think I've reached a point in my life where I can tell between nougat and cookie. So let's not just say things that we both know are obvious fabrications.
jokes.setup: Mechanic suggesting it's nougat
jokes.punchline: George's declaration of candy expertise and accusation of fabrication
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - George treating candy identification as serious expertise. 'Obvious fabrications' elevates the stakes perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not nougat! You're making this up! I know candy. I know the five main food groups: candy, candy canes, candy corn, and syrup.

jokes.text: You know you're getting a little vein there? I know about the vein. This guy.
jokes.setup: Mechanic pointing out George's stress vein
jokes.punchline: George's resigned acknowledgment of his vein
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the vein gag. George's weary familiarity with his own stress response is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: You see that vein?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know about the vein.

jokes.text: There's no law in this place. Anything goes. It's Thunderdome.
jokes.setup: George describing the dealership chaos
jokes.punchline: The Mad Max Thunderdome reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation comparing dealership to post-apocalyptic wasteland. 'It's Thunderdome' is incredibly economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was like Mad Max Thunderdome out there! Two men enter, one man leaves!

jokes.text: Well, I almost did once and I blacked out. When I came to the car was in a ditch and the tank was full. I don't know who did it. And I never got to thank them.
jokes.setup: Kramer describing going below empty on gas gauge
jokes.punchline: The mysterious good Samaritan who filled his tank while he was unconscious
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kramer story with perfect absurdist logic. The mysterious benefactor and inability to thank them is wonderfully strange.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I coasted into a gas station on fumes. I'm passed out on the steering wheel, and when I come to, the tank is full!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Someone filled your tank?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah! Some good Samaritan. I never saw who it was.

jokes.text: George. Vein it up.
jokes.setup: Jerry calling George to negotiate
jokes.punchline: Encouraging George to use his anger/vein as a weapon
jokes.explanation: 'Vein it up' is perfectly economical and turns George's stress into a tactical advantage. Great callback usage.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you gotta use the vein. You know, when you get angry, that vein in your forehead pops out. That's your weapon.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm serious! Women find that intimidating. Next time someone upsets you, just let that vein do the talking.

jokes.text: I'm starving. I can feel my stomach sucking up against my spine.
jokes.setup: George expressing his hunger
jokes.punchline: The graphic description of internal organs
jokes.explanation: Vivid George exaggeration with good visual imagery. Character-appropriate dramatic hunger description.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm starving. My stomach is eating itself.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not possible.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My pancreas is filing a lawsuit against my liver. My kidneys have already retained counsel.

jokes.text: Oh, I never felt so alive. All right, I'm satisfied. We better get some gas. What? Well, we can't stop now. What do you mean? We have to keep going to the dealership. That was the plan.
jokes.setup: Kramer feeling alive from driving on empty
jokes.punchline: His refusal to get gas and commitment to the dangerous plan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer escalation - becoming addicted to the danger. The commitment to 'the plan' shows his stubborn logic.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm alive, baby! I'm alive!

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: Kramer, you need to get gas.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No! No, I'm not stopping now. I've got a good feeling about this.

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: You're going to run out of gas in the middle of nowhere!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's the beauty of it, Rick. That's the beauty.

jokes.text: Let's just go for it like Thelma and Louise. They drove to a dealership? No, they drove off a cliff.
jokes.setup: Kramer's Thelma and Louise comparison
jokes.punchline: Rick's correction about their actual fate
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Rick's literal correction of Kramer's romanticized movie reference. Dark undertones make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's like Thelma and Louise.

dialogue.speaker: Rick
dialogue.line: They drove off a cliff.

jokes.text: You are one sick mama. I like it.
jokes.setup: Rick's correction about Thelma and Louise
jokes.punchline: Kramer's appreciation for the dark implication
jokes.explanation: Reveals Kramer's attraction to danger and darkness. The casual 'I like it' is perfectly Kramer-like boundary crossing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, they didn't jump. They drove off a cliff. There's a difference.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's what I'm saying - they went out on their own terms. No one made them do it.

jokes.text: You know, just that you cared enough to call means so much, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Jerry calling Elaine to get back with Puddy for his car deal
jokes.punchline: Elaine's sarcastic appreciation for Jerry's 'caring'
jokes.explanation: Good sarcasm from Elaine recognizing Jerry's selfish motives. Character-appropriate cynicism about friendship.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You gotta get back with Puddy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? Why would I do that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's got a great deal on a car. You'd be doing me a favor.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, so let me get this straight. You want me to get back together with a man I broke up with so you can save a few hundred dollars on a car?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: When you say it like that, it sounds bad.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh no, that's very sweet. That's really considerate of you. I'm gonna go cry now.

jokes.text: Yeah, right. I'm gonna get my car repaired at a dealership. Why don't I just flush my money down the toilet?
jokes.setup: Manager asking when George brought his car in
jokes.punchline: George's dramatic comparison of dealership service to flushing money
jokes.explanation: Classic George escalation with vivid money-wasting metaphor. Shows his deep cynicism about car dealerships.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: When did you bring your car in?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Three weeks ago.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: Three weeks? What have you been doing?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What have I been doing? I've been watching you people throw my money down the toilet! It's like flushing hundred dollar bills!

jokes.text: I caught him and his face was covered in chocolate and cookie crumbs. I thought you said it was a Twix? Oh, it was. But he claimed it was a 5th Avenue bar. Maybe it was. Oh, no. No. Twix is the only candy with the cookie crunch.
jokes.setup: George explaining his Twix theft case
jokes.punchline: His insistence on Twix's unique cookie crunch properties
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of George's candy forensics expertise. The cookie crunch detail becomes his key evidence.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The thing about a Twix is the cookie. It's got a snap to it. A crunch. When you bite into a Twix, you hear a crunch. That's what makes it special.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: Mr. Costanza, you stole a candy bar.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I didn't steal it. I forgot to pay for it. But the point is, the Twix has this unique cookie structure that you don't get with other bars. It's not just chocolate. It's the combination of the caramel, the chocolate, and that cookie crunch.

jokes.text: All I want is my 75 cents back, an apology, and for him to be fired.
jokes.setup: George's demands for justice
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 75 cents to firing someone
jokes.explanation: Perfect George escalation - the jump from small monetary loss to career destruction is hilariously disproportionate.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I want justice! This coffee cost 75 cents and it's not even hot!

dialogue.speaker: Cashier
dialogue.line: Sir, it's just coffee...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Just coffee?! Do you know who I am? I'm George Costanza. I demand to speak to the manager.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: What seems to be the problem?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The problem is your employee sold me cold coffee. I think they should be fired.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: Fired? Over 75 cents?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not about the money. It's about principle. Standards! If we let this slide, what's next? Cold soup? Stale donuts? Chaos!

jokes.text: Twix has too much coconut. No, there's no coconut! I'm allergic to coconut. I'm not.
jokes.setup: Old man's random Twix comment
jokes.punchline: The confusion about non-existent coconut in Twix
jokes.explanation: Random old man confusion adds to the chaos but doesn't contribute much structurally. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Manager's father
dialogue.line: It's a Twix. You have two sticks. Two for me, one for you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? There's no coconut in a Twix.

dialogue.speaker: Manager's father
dialogue.line: Of course there's coconut! It's a Twix!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, there's caramel and chocolate. That's it. No coconut.

dialogue.speaker: Manager's father
dialogue.line: You're crazy. I've been eating Twix my whole life. There's definitely coconut.

jokes.text: Clearly, no one else can stand to be with either one of you. I guess. Good point.
jokes.setup: Jerry mediating Elaine and Puddy's relationship
jokes.punchline: His brutally honest assessment that they're both unbearable
jokes.explanation: Brilliant relationship counseling that's brutally honest instead of supportive. Their acceptance makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look, I've been thinking about you two, and I have to be honest with you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're both unbearable. Elaine, you're critical and overbearing. Puddy, you're dense and annoying.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You deserve each other.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Excuse me?

dialogue.speaker: Puddy
dialogue.line: Yeah, what's your problem, man?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My problem? You're my problem. Both of you.

jokes.text: Now, what do I have to do to put you two in a relationship today?
jokes.setup: Jerry mediating relationship issues
jokes.punchline: Using car salesman closing language for relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect parody of car sales closing techniques applied to relationships. Economical and brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're having issues with your girlfriend?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look, here's what you do. You use the car salesman close. You say, 'I can get you into this relationship for the same monthly payment as your last one.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Then you throw in the undercoating - that's the couple's massage package.

jokes.text: When that car rolls into that dealership and that tank is bone-dry, I want you to be there with me when everyone says, 'Kramer and that other guy... they went farther to the left of the slash than anyone ever dreamed.'
jokes.setup: Kramer's motivational speech about running on empty
jokes.punchline: The grandiose vision of their empty-tank legacy
jokes.explanation: Epic Kramer speech turning mundane gas gauge reading into heroic legend. 'Left of the slash' is brilliantly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, we're living in a world of bladders, Jerry. A world of bladders. But some men, some men choose to run on empty. They don't need the petty concerns of a full tank. They're free. They're alive. And when they finally sputter to a stop on some desolate highway, their legacy lives on. Not in the things they accomplished, but in the sheer audacity of their emptiness.

jokes.text: That took you an hour? Only I hold the answer key to their true candy identities.
jokes.setup: George's elaborate candy lineup preparation
jokes.punchline: His dramatic declaration about holding the 'answer key'
jokes.explanation: Perfect George obsession with treating candy identification as a CSI investigation. 'Answer key' elevates the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I got it. I got the answer key. I'm gonna hold it right here. And I'm gonna make them sweat.

jokes.text: Hey, Willie, check it out, free candy. That's my candy lineup. Where are my cards? They're all on the floor.
jokes.setup: George's carefully prepared candy lineup
jokes.punchline: Random employees destroying his elaborate investigation
jokes.explanation: Perfect destruction of George's elaborate plan by oblivious employees. Classic sitcom frustration escalation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I've arranged these candies in a very specific order for a reason.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: What reason?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: To determine which one is the best. Now, nobody touch anything.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: Got it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm serious. This is a scientific study.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: We'll leave it alone.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: Hey, these look good!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No! Stop! You've ruined everything!

jokes.text: Like, eight Twix? No. Hey, this Clark bar is good. It's a Twix. They're all Twix. It was a setup. A setup, I tell you. And you've robbed it. You've all screwed me again.
jokes.setup: The candy lineup being consumed
jokes.punchline: George's revelation that it was all Twix and his paranoid accusations
jokes.explanation: Brilliant revelation that ruins George's plan while proving his point. 'A setup, I tell you' is perfectly dramatic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. These are all Twix.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You sold me a box of all Twix!

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So? So you can't just put all the same candy in a box. That's false advertising!

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: It's a Twix box.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I thought there was variety! I thought I was getting a nice assortment!

jokes.text: Twix!
jokes.setup: George's complete frustration with the Twix situation
jokes.punchline: His final desperate scream of 'Twix!'
jokes.explanation: Perfect culmination of George's Twix obsession in one primal scream. Economical and emotionally satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Twix!

jokes.text: We did it. We pulled it off. I can't believe it. Where's the needle? Oh, it broke off, baby.
jokes.setup: Arriving at dealership on empty tank
jokes.punchline: The gas gauge needle broke off from going so low
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - they pushed the gas gauge beyond its limits. 'Broke off, baby' is triumphant and destructive.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The gas gauge needle broke off from going so low.

jokes.text: You couldn't just give him one high-five? And where does it end? Then everyone's doing it. It's like the wave at ball games. Air quotes. The phrase, 'Don't go there.' Someone's gotta take a stand.
jokes.setup: Elaine questioning Jerry's high-five refusal
jokes.punchline: Jerry's slippery slope argument about cultural phenomena
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation connecting high-fives to cultural decline. The examples are perfect and 'someone's gotta take a stand' is heroic.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why won't you give me a high-five?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because once you start doing high-fives, where does it end? Next thing you know, you're doing the wave at baseball games, you're wearing a foam finger, you're going to monster truck rallies.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Is it? Is it really?

jokes.text: I don't know. All of them, they're all crooks. I couldn't get all different candy bars anyway.
jokes.setup: Being asked who he was trying to setup
jokes.punchline: Admitting his plan was flawed from the start
jokes.explanation: George's paranoid worldview and admission of planning failure. Shows his chaotic thought process.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, I was trying to set up Elaine with... you know what? I don't even know what I was thinking. The whole thing was ridiculous from the beginning.

jokes.text: A nickel.
jokes.setup: Episode ending
jokes.punchline: Old man's random price memory
jokes.explanation: Weak ending callback to earlier candy price discussion. Functions as a button but doesn't add much.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Costanza
dialogue.line: I remember when a loaf of bread was a nickel.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I remember when you had hair.

tmdb_overview: At a car dealership, Kramer takes a test drive, while Jerry's deal is threatened by a fight between Elaine and Puddy.
insight: Escalation comedy drives 84-point episode: 61 jokes in 24 minutes sustain absurdist car-lot chaos.
